posts.name: Rahsaan Maxwell
posts.cooked:
I’ve heard a lot about Boudignon. And despite my general reluctance to spend that much money when great chenin can be had for much less, the style sounded so appealing that I gave in and bought these wines. Both at a digestible 12%.

2022 Boudignon Savennières Clos de Fremine
Definitely a new idiom of Savennières. Neither the old backwards style nor the pumped up modern style. This showed all the pure clear delicate elegance that I was expecting. Not even sure I would pick it as Savennières blind. The herbal flinty elements made me think of Chablis/Muscadet/Sancerre. As it opens it does show more of the chenin varietal notes. But whatever it is, it is silky elegant deliciousness that convinced me.

2021 Boudignon Savennières Clos de la Hutte
Was expecting a step up, but maybe not the best timing. Even more delicate and more gentle, with the added bonus of the sharper 2021 vintage that leaned sour in comparison. Needs a lot of air to kick into gear, and does become a very nice wine as the midpalate arrives at the party. Not sure if the vintage comparison was too challenging, or if this will just be a lot better with more time. It’s certainly a very delicate and elegant wine, so I’ll give it respect. But this showing did not offer that much more pleasure than the 22 Fremine, for whatever that is worth.

posts.name: Gary Schulte
posts.cooked: For myself the Boudignon pricing had me move away from his wines. In the past I’ve had a few that I loved and a couple that were out of balance with too much acidity. One of those acidic wines I have stored away for a later comparison with other chenin blancs. If his pricing was more on par with other distinctive dry chenins I would probably still be sampling Boudignon.

posts.name: Rahsaan Maxwell
posts.cooked: Yes, I can see that!

posts.name: Justin Izzo
posts.cooked: I have a 2020 Clos de Frémine on the list to try in the next few weeks. Will be sure to post notes here. Been interested in trying Boudignon for a while but have read here and there about very high acidity in some of his wines, which isn’t quite my style. That’s probably kept me from diving in sooner.

posts.name: Rahsaan Maxwell
posts.cooked: 2020 should provide you some body and weight. But the style should still be apparent, and if that’s not your thing, then probably not something for you to keep chasing.

posts.name: Jacob Goldman
posts.cooked: I have the higher level cuvees stored away (was told to wait), but I think his Anjou blanc is excellent value. Who out there produces something both better and cheaper?

posts.name: Rahsaan Maxwell
posts.cooked:
jdvgoldman:

…I think his Anjou blanc is excellent value. Who out there produces something both better and cheaper?

I haven’t had the Anjou blanc, I see it retails for around $50 in the US. That’s a lot cheaper than Hutte/Fremine, but if we’re comparing chenin, that’s also the price for the top Vouvray/Montlouis. Even in Savennieres, top wines from Epire, Aux Moines are that price, and I’m not sure ‘quality’ is the main differential. Perhaps style.

Regardless, definitely nice wines I will continue to drink. Will just have to see how they fit into the priority list!

posts.name: Tony Gibson
posts.cooked:
I imported the wines with the 2011 vintage. At the time I think he only had two or maybe three different wines. They were considerably less expensive than today. IIRC the Anjou would have retailed for about $30 and A Francoise around $40. Very precise wine making. It’s been interesting to watch the following develop. When I introduced the wines to market practically no one was familiar with the name in the US. Most buyers just kind of shrugged their shoulders upon tasting and not many bought. I was beginning to scale down my business with the release of the 2012s and passed on them since the 2011 was hardly selling. A few months later the wines developed a following after a couple notable somms started talking them up on Instagram. It was wild to see somms suddenly asking to buy whatever we had left in stock.

I love Chenin but the wines were always just a little austere for personal preference. Love the tension he packs in there and wondered if they might round out over time but still carry energy. Had my last 2011 maybe two years ago. It was still holding on just fine but, for my taste, still on the austere side and losing some fruit. Seemed like it was gracefully fading into the sunset. Really good wines but considering where prices are today, there are many other options I like better.

posts.name: Rahsaan Maxwell
posts.cooked:
TGibson:

I imported the wines with the 2011 vintage…

Thanks for that. Yes, it’s amazing how that whole somm/instagram dynamic works…

Your perspective is good. But to be fair, Hutte and Fremine are supposedly big steps up in terms of vineyard quality from what he was working with in 2011? Although the vines are super young! (Hutte planted 2011 and Fremine planted 2015. Crazy…)

posts.name: Tony Gibson
posts.cooked: I haven’t tried those. He does seem very focused on quality so not surprising he was on the hunt for better sites. He was a Judo champ (similar to Jean Masson in the Savoie who I also represented) and I think that attention to detail is apparent in the wines. At the time of the inaugural release he was still making wine for Rosenthal’s producer. I forget the name. His personal project was basically a side job at the time.

posts.name: J@son Tr@ughber
posts.cooked:
Rahsaan_M:

I haven’t had the Anjou blanc, I see it retails for around $50 in the US

That’s pain. £28 for me in the UK. Though hard to get allocated more than a couple of his higher-end wines, which are much higher priced.

posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked: Who loves Boudignon?
posts.display_username: Brian G r a f s t r o m

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Yeah, me too.

I love the basic Anjou Blanc and in the steelier vintages like 2016, 2017, and 2021 I tend to buy this in cases and then try to add 3-6 bottles of the upper tier wines. Pricing has gotten challenging for those compared to 5-6 years ago, but while I enjoy other Savenierre and Anjou producers Boudignon is my favorite. His acidity suits my palate, and even in the riper vintages like 2018 & 2019 the wines are very well made.

reply_to_user.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m

posts.name: John Maguire
posts.cooked: I love Boudignon. Personally I find a big step up in quality between the Anjou Blanc and the Fremine/Huttes. Agree that it’s a particular style, one that I greatly enjoy.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: He is a real perfectionist and one of the very finest growers in the Loire, and indeed in France. In fact, I have been trying to add more to my cellar over the last six months. For me, the best producer of dry Chenin today.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

He is a real perfectionist and one of the very finest growers in the Loire, and indeed in France. In fact, I have been trying to add more to my cellar over the last six months. For me, the best producer of dry Chenin today.

I like them very much but they can also be extremely pricey. I just found a bottle of the 2021 Franc de Pied and it’s $265 USD!

I will say the basic Anjou Blanc is mighty fine and well priced, generally around $40-$50.

posts.name: Jim Stewart
posts.cooked:
Gary_Schulte:

other distinctive dry chenins

Gary, I would be interested in some of your recommendations. Thanks.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
I think he was one of the first to try the “new” Anjou/Savennières style, taut and crisp, of which I’ve become a great fan. I’ve only tried three - the Anjou, A Françoise and Clos de la Hutte - all very enjoyable, but none of them blew me away, after factoring in the price. For me, that’s the problem - I’ve had as much pleasure from several Ogereau or Plaisance wines, not to mention Pierre Ménard, which were all a little to a lot cheaper.
I don’t begrudge his success in the least - it’s thoroughly deserved and the prices also reflect the small size of the vineyard, plus the cost of using frost protection. He was badly hit by frost and bought an expensive electric heating system.
I think his wines are definitely among the best in the region, but I just wish they weren’t so expensive.

posts.name: D@vid K@ntr0witz
posts.cooked: I’m a fan. One of the more successful “new wave” Loire chenin producers IMO. His wines actually have enough fruit to balance the high acidity.

posts.name: Rahsaan Maxwell
posts.cooked:
David_K2:

I’m a fan. One of the more successful “new wave” Loire chenin producers IMO. His wines actually have enough fruit to balance the high acidity.

I guess it depends which crew you consider the new wave. I still think of Laureau and Morgat and that generation as the new wave of Savennieres, moving away from the austere backwards wines of the 90s. And in many cases they went too far towards plush fruit for my tastes.

I see Boudignon as an attractive ‘new wave’ producer precisely because he doesn’t lean too much on the fruit.

If we’re talking about Anjou more broadly, I suppose that’s a different story. Yes, it did used to be broader and looser.
